challenges.title: Swap Variables
challenges.description: Given `a = 10` and `b = 20`, swap their values so that `a` becomes 20 and `b` becomes 10. Use Python's tuple unpacking to do this in a single line — no temporary variable allowed!
challenges.starter_code:
a = 10
b = 20

# Swap a and b using tuple unpacking (one line)
challenges.test_code:
assert a == 20, f'Expected a to be 20 after swap, got {a}'
assert b == 10, f'Expected b to be 10 after swap, got {b}'
hints: Python lets you assign multiple variables at once: x, y = y, x
The right side is evaluated first, then assigned to the left side.
No parentheses are needed — the comma creates the tuple.

challenges.title: Extended Unpacking
challenges.description: Given `data = [1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7]`, use Python's extended unpacking (the * operator) to assign the first element to `first`, the last element to `last`, and everything in between to `middle`. You should end up with first=1, middle=[2,3,4,5,6], last=7.
challenges.starter_code:
data = [1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7]

# Use extended unpacking to split data into first, middle, and last
challenges.test_code:
assert first == 1, f'Expected first to be 1, got {first}'
assert middle == [2, 3, 4, 5, 6], f'Expected middle to be [2,3,4,5,6], got {middle}'
assert last == 7, f'Expected last to be 7, got {last}'
assert data == [1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7], 'Original data should not be modified'
hints: The * operator in an assignment collects 'remaining' elements into a list.
You can place the starred variable in any position: first, middle, or last.
The syntax is: first, *middle, last = sequence
